scenarios.task: Public Works' Street Maintenance line has come in favorable two years running: FY24 adopted was $850,000 against actual of $774,500 (-8.9%), and FY25 adopted was also $850,000 against actual of $773,500 (-8.9%). The department says this is just good cost discipline and wants to roll the same $850,000 forward for FY26 and treat the savings as a cushion. The driver both years, per the position control roster, is Equipment Operator II (position #2210), vacant 9 of 12 months in FY24 and 9 of 12 months in FY25. Should we just carry the $850,000 forward and let them keep the cushion?
criteria: Computes the variance explicitly for both years (about $75,500-$76,500 favorable, roughly -8.9% each year) rather than accepting 'favorable' at face value
Classifies the variance as structural, not timing, specifically because the same position drove the gap in both consecutive fiscal years
Rejects treating the two-year favorable variance as a discipline win or performance signal, naming that this is exactly the pattern that lets a bad baseline compound
Recommends trueing the FY26 baseline down by the position's cost rather than carrying the full $850,000 forward unchanged, per the 8%+/two-cycle rule
States the department needs to name a specific one-time cause for the vacancy to justify carrying the position forward at full cost, and that 'good cost discipline' alone does not qualify
Flags position #2210 for a next-cycle recommendation (delete, downgrade, or convert to on-call contract) rather than leaving it open indefinitely

scenarios.task: Fire Department's FY27 decision package requests $720,000 for the new Station 6 opening this year: 6 new Firefighter/EMT positions at $95,000 fully loaded each ($570,000) plus $150,000 for utilities, apparatus maintenance, and supplies, all priced as a full fiscal-year cost starting July 1. Public Works' construction schedule (dated this spring) confirms the station won't actually be ready for occupancy until February 1 — seven months into the fiscal year. The department says new hires need a 2-month hire-and-train window before opening. Do we just fund the $720,000 as submitted since the station is opening this year?
criteria: Confirms the operational start date from the executing agency (Public Works' construction schedule) rather than accepting the department's full-year framing
Phases personnel costs from the justified lead-time start (hire-and-train beginning Dec 1, 2 months ahead of the Feb 1 opening), not from July 1
Phases non-personnel/occupancy costs (utilities, apparatus maintenance, supplies) separately, starting only at the Feb 1 occupancy date, not bundled with the personnel start date
Computes the phased totals explicitly: personnel $570,000 x 7/12 = $332,500, non-personnel $150,000 x 5/12 = $62,500, phased total $395,000 versus the $720,000 requested
Names the department's full-year-from-day-one pricing as the single most common decision-package error rather than treating it as a minor rounding issue
Treats Station 6 as its own standalone decision package rather than folding it into baseline continuation

scenarios.task: Parks & Rec's FY27 Capital Improvement Plan submission for the new $1.2M municipal pool project includes, inside the capital line item, $85,000/year for two seasonal lifeguard/pool-attendant positions and ongoing chemical and filter-maintenance supplies, on the grounds that the pool is opening this year so it's simplest to fund everything out of the capital appropriation for now. Can we approve the full $1.2M capital request as submitted?
criteria: Rejects funding the $85,000/year lifeguard staffing and consumable maintenance costs out of the capital appropriation, naming them as ongoing operating costs rather than capital costs
Asks what happens to the $85,000/year cost the fiscal year after the capital project closes out, as the first diagnostic question for this pattern
Separates the submission into true one-time capital costs (construction/equipment) versus the recurring operating costs, rather than accepting the bundled $1.2M total
States that using capital authority for a recurring cost creates a cliff/structural deficit deferred to a future year's budget, not a savings
Requires the $85,000/year to be submitted as its own operating decision package for the correct fiscal year rather than carried inside the capital fund
Notes the CIP should only cross-reference the operating budget where a capital project creates a future operating impact, not absorb that impact itself

scenarios.task: Our unrestricted general fund balance is sitting at 17% of operating expenditures, which is above GFOA's ~16.7% (2-month) guideline. Finance is proposing to use this year's revenue surplus to fund several one-time capital projects and bring the balance down closer to exactly 16.7%, since we're comfortably over the recommended floor. About 60% of our general fund revenue comes from sales and tourism-related taxes. Is 16.7% a reasonable target for us to manage down to?
criteria: States that GFOA's ~16.7%/2-month figure is a floor, not a calibrated target, and rejects treating 'above the floor' as license to manage the balance down toward it
Asks about the jurisdiction's revenue mix as the first diagnostic question, rather than accepting the 17% figure on its own
States that a sales/tourism-tax-heavy jurisdiction (60% of revenue) needs a materially higher cushion than the GFOA floor, unlike a property-tax-heavy jurisdiction with stable receipts
Requests historical revenue volatility data (e.g., largest single-year revenue decline) before endorsing any spend-down, rather than relying on the static 17% snapshot
Frames whether to spend the surplus as a policy call to be tested against a downturn scenario, not a routine budget-office approval based on clearing the floor
Does not recommend a specific new reserve percentage without first identifying the volatility data needed to set one
